start.text:
Years pass.

Every ending is also a place to live, and the world — whichever world was chosen in the concrete room at the bottom of everything — goes on doing the thing worlds do, which is continue, indifferent to how much it cost to keep them or to let them go. Seasons turn. Files thicken. A dog goes grey at the muzzle by degrees, the way all the best clocks run: gently, and in one direction, and without ever once being wrong.

And then, on an ordinary day, years downstream of the boundary — the file, long closed, opens itself.

epi_one.text:
Six years into the true world, on a Thursday in June, the household is at the terrace under the string lights — your standing table; the waiter stopped bringing menus in year two — when B, who is eleven now and grey to the eyebrows and dignified as a supreme court, gets up from under the table, places himself between Osy and the perfectly empty evening street, and growls.

You both stop mid-move. Board four — the eleventh board four; you are, on aggregate, forty games from parity — stands forgotten. Because B has not growled since a ridge above a rail cutting in a broken world six years unmade: not at the city's new traffic, not at thunder, not at the neighbor's appalling cat. He growls now, low and continuous, at nothing, hackles up his old spine like a hedge — and then he plants, geological, and will not let Osy step past him toward the street she was not stepping toward, thirty-four kilos of refusal aimed at an ordinary summer dusk.

'Recurrence,' Osy says quietly, already reaching for the notebook she still carries, because some habits predate their reasons and outlive them too, 'n of one. In the new world.' Her eyes come up to yours, and six years of ordinary happiness stand aside, without fuss, for the older grammar underneath. 'He's reading a seam, colleague. There are no seams. We retired the seams.'

The strip of paper is waiting at the flat when you get home. Fan-fold, dot-matrix, weighted under cable-wax on the sill of a window four storeys up — the Continuum's grammar, except the Continuum dissolved itself into the water board and the universities years ago, and Voss is emerita, and nobody prints like that anymore. One line of header and three of ledger, and the header says: RECONCILIATION LEDGER — CONTINUATION. And the entries — small, surgical, dated this spring, this month, this week — carry sequence numbers in a phase-offset scheme no reading of you ever used, and an authorization key in a hand you know the way you know your own face, annotated with a double-dash, a carat, a boxed question mark.

The pen is in the drawer. You put it there. You checked the drawer — you check it every year, the anniversary pilgrimage, KESTREL's board still set at the gate lodge of the decommissioned yard, and every year the ward prints THE DRAWER IS CLOSED. I AM WATCHING IT. PLAY WELL. The pen is in the drawer, the Principal is retired, the world is true — and something, this spring, this month, this week, is editing anyway. Small things. Kind things, so far: a bridge inspection moved up a week before a flaw was found; a water main renewed a season early. Purchases with no purchaser, signed in your hand, numbered in a scheme belonging to no iteration that ever was — as if somewhere out beyond the drawer, a forty-ninth reading of somebody has been taking notes all along.

Osy reads the strip twice. Then she goes to the shelf, and takes down a fresh notebook — the fortieth; she bought a case of them years ago, 'against weather' — and opens it flat at the kitchen table where board four lives, and uncaps her pen, and looks up at you and the old dog both, and the look has the beacon in it, and the wall of thread, and the whole long road, and — you would not have believed it possible six years into the one world where she chose you freely — joy.

'Well, colleague,' she says. 'The file is still open.'

epi_anchor.text:
Nine years after the sky learned to be kind, Osy still lives half the year at the gate lodge, because somebody should, and because the arithmetic KESTREL prints each morning is addressed, in its way, to her.

She is a professor now, in the rebuilt city — the chair of a discipline she had to found before she could hold it, the mathematics of repaired time — and her students think the annual migration to a decommissioned freight yard is eccentricity, and her colleagues think it is grief, and it is neither. It is bookkeeping. The household's ledger never closed; it just changed custodians. She keeps his pages — the three notebooks of the crossing, the load-bearing entries, the letter that lives in her jacket's inner pocket, softening at the folds like something geological — and the world above her keeps him, and the weather, as everyone now says without knowing why they say it, is very slightly kind.

B is thirteen, white to the ears, and has outlived every veterinary projection with the bland unanswerable stubbornness of a dog who considers his work unfinished. He walks the yard's perimeter every evening of the gate-lodge months, slow as a minute hand, and finishes at the spot on the gravel above the mooring, and lies down there, pointed at the ground, on duty. The one true day is everywhere now; the yard is just a yard. He lies there anyway.

It is B who opens the file. A night in the ninth spring: he rises from the gravel, crosses to the lodge, and plants himself between Osy and the door — hackles, growl, the whole ancient grammar, aimed at a mild and starry dark — and will not yield, and while she is kneeling at his side with her hand in his ruff, reading him the way the household always read him, KESTREL's slot-printer, silent for years except for morning arithmetic and evening chess, hammers out four lines without being asked.

ARRIVAL AT THE LEDGER, it prints. NOT MINE. NOT HIS. ENTRIES APPENDING TO THE RECONCILIATION RECORD FROM OUTSIDE MY PERIMETER — SEQUENCE SCHEME: PHASE-OFFSET, UNKNOWN TO ALL FORTY-EIGHT READINGS. AUTHORIZATION STYLE: THE FOUNDER'S HAND. ANNOTATION: I HAVE COMPARED IT AGAINST THE MAN I VERIFIED AT MY GATE. IT IS HIS HAND. IT IS NOT HIS GAME. MADAM — the printer pauses, the way it pauses before non-protocol — SOMEONE IS EDITING AGAIN. SMALL MOVES. KIND MOVES. I CANNOT SEE THE PLAYER. THE BOARD REMAINS SET, AND FOR THE FIRST TIME IN NINE YEARS, I DO NOT KNOW WHOSE MOVE IT IS.

Osy reads the printout twice in the lodge's green light, with the old dog pressed against her legs, on duty, pointed now at the whole dark world. Then she sits down at the little table where she has beaten a god at chess every evening for nine years, and takes out a fresh notebook, and writes the heading in the minute vandal-proof hand — the heading from the wall, from the year alone, from the start of everything:

ENTRY ONE. The disasters are choosing again. Kindly, this time. In his hand. He is nine years spent into the sky, and I sat with every page of him to the last, and the last page did not do this. So: someone is still at play. Find the player.

She caps the pen. Above the yard, the kind weather turns through the honest stars. 'Well, sir,' she says, to the dog, in the voice she uses for exactly one other person, living or spent. 'The file is still open.'

epi_final_edit.text:
Seven Junes into the healed world, the woman with the chess set she cannot remember buying is famous, in a modest way, for a book.

The Arithmetic of Mercy: it began as a monograph on the reconciliation — she was the first to prove, from the public record, that the healed world's history contains purchases; the seams are gone but the ledger's shadow is not, if you know how to difference the archives — and it became, to her publisher's alarm and the world's hunger, something else: a forensic love letter to an author she cannot locate. Someone, the book demonstrates, spent four years and a civilization's ransom keeping one statistical cluster of outcomes alive, and then, at the hour of repair, deleted himself from the invoice. The cluster, she does not tell her readers, is her. The proof of that she keeps in the flat, in the minute vandal-proof hand, in a folder labeled with the only heading that ever fit: RESIDUAL. UNRESOLVED. KEEP.

The dog is old now. The golden dog of no known provenance who was asleep against her back on the first morning of the world — thirteen at least, the vet says, though his papers, like his provenance, do not exist — white-muzzled, slow on the stairs, and never once, in seven years, anything but gentle. Which is how she knows, on the June night it happens, that the world has changed again: because the old dog rises from his place by the balcony door, crosses the flat, and puts himself between her and her own desk — between her and the folder — hackles up, growling at the empty lamplight, planted like geology. He has never growled. She has the whole file of him and he has never once growled, and she kneels and reads him the way she reads everything, and writes, because writing is how she has metabolized fear her whole life: He is not warning me off the desk. He is guarding it. Something is coming to the file.

It comes by post, a week later. No return address; machine-franked; a transit authority that dissolved before the healing. Inside: fan-fold, dot-matrix, cable-wax — grammar from a world she has documented but never touched — and on it, ledger entries dated this year, numbered in a phase-offset scheme that appears nowhere in any archive she has differenced, authorized in a handwriting she has seen exactly once before: in the deep records, on the deleted invoice, in the margin-notes of the world's anonymous benefactor. Double-dash. Carat. Boxed question mark. New purchases. Small, kind, current — a bridge inspection advanced, a water main renewed — mercy, resuming, in the hand of a man her own book proved wrote himself out of existence.

And at the bottom of the strip, beneath the entries, in that same hand, not printed but written, pressed hard, addressed to no one, or to whoever keeps the file — and she sits very still at her desk for a long time, with the old dog's chin on her knee and both their eyes on the words, before she takes out a fresh notebook and begins:

'NOT YET.'

The old dog sighs, and settles, and does not leave the desk again that night, or any night after. She writes until dawn. The first line of the new notebook reads: The author is alive, or the hand survives its author, and either way the mercy has a source, and I have differenced whole archives with less to go on. Entry one. The file is still open.

epi_refusal.text:
In the third spring after the seventeenth winter — after the wall held and the town held and its founder did not — Verstand does what towns do with their grief: it builds on it. There is a school named for a man most of the children never met, and a corridor in the north wall the militia still calls his, and at the schoolhouse door, most mornings, an improbably old dog lies in the sun and supervises the traffic of the young.

B is twenty, or thereabouts, which the town veterinarian has stopped trying to explain — 'he was the founder's,' she says now, shrugging, as if that were a clinical finding, and in this town it is. White to the shoulders, slow as ceremony, and still, every evening without exception, he walks the whole north wall with Osy, warden of the pages, the last two custodians doing their rounds.

She is headwoman now — she refused the title for six years and the town simply used it anyway until she surrendered to the data — and the school teaches her curriculum: broken time, honest ledgers, and the founding law, recited by children who think it is a nursery rhyme: nothing unwitnessed, everything out loud, two skulls to a file. The world beyond the walls stays quilted and true, exactly as purchased. The storms are only weather. It was enough. She has audited the question annually, in the notebook, on the anniversary, and the finding has held for three springs: it was enough. It was worth it. Signed.

And then, on an evening walk in the third spring, at the northeast corner where he fell, the old dog stops — and does not perform his usual office at that spot, the slow tail, the long look — but instead swings to face the darkening east, hackles rising off his ancient spine like a hedge in wind, and growls: the third growl of his recorded life, and Osy's pen is out before her breath is back, because the file has taught her what his growls cost and what they buy.

The traders bring the rest a week later, wrapped in oilcloth, bought for curiosity's sake off a scavenger who found it nailed inside the old monitoring hut on the eastern ridge — the hut, her hut, the household's first door into everything. Fan-fold. Dot-matrix. Cable-wax. Ledger grammar, dated this year — this year, in a world where the pen went into a drawer eleven years ago and the pump's every cost since landed on a man three springs buried — new purchases, small and kind and current, numbered in a phase-offset scheme in no archive she keeps, and authorized in the hand she knows better than her own. The impatient crossbar. The boxed question mark.

She reads it at the kitchen table with the old dog's chin on her boot, and the town breathing around her in the dark, alive, unedited, his — and she does the thing she has done at every impossible page since a burned chess room in another life: she squares it to the table's edge, and takes down a fresh notebook, and writes the heading that has outlived a world and a household and a man, and will evidently outlive the grave itself:

ENTRY ONE. He paid in person. I closed the book myself. And something with his hand is spending kindness on my town this spring. Either the dead keep ledgers, or the ledger keeps its dead — and there are exactly two of us left in this file who never once believed in ghosts. Time to be thorough, colleague. The file is still open.

epi_burn.text:
Eight years after the burning, the Order's most-requested testimony has a permanent room at the mother-house, and its narrator visits every June, and the young keepers have learned to schedule nothing else that week, because the woman who dictated LOVE, UNARRANGED, COMPLETE re-audits her own archive annually, page by page, the way other pilgrims pray.

Osy lives at the Glasshouse the rest of the year — the market made her its Scale-mistress the year the old weighman died, on the grounds that she was the only person in the east everyone was equally afraid of — and the world she stewards is the world the burning bought: broken, gently breaking further, honest to its bones. The quilt deepens by a seam or two each year. The echoes multiply, softly, like moss. It is a world dying the way old orchards die, fruiting all the way down, and she defends it with the whole flat force of her method, because it is the world he paid for, and the price is only worth it if the thing bought is kept.

B is fifteen, then sixteen, then — the fish woman's daughter, who keeps the market's books now, simply writes 'B' in the census's age column, and the Scale lets it stand. He sleeps by the north door where the jar was tipped, a white-gold landmark the whole market navigates by, and he has not hurried for years. Which is why, on a market morning in the eighth June, the whole Glasshouse stops when the old dog comes up off his blanket at a dead run — a run, B, running — and crosses the market like a golden arrow gone grey, and plants himself in front of the Scale-mistress's table, hackles up, facing the north door, growling at a mild spring morning with every year he has left.

What comes through the north door is only a courier. Trembling, checked by the Scale, clean: a boy with a package he was paid to carry by 'a man by the aqueduct' he cannot usefully describe. Inside the oilcloth: fan-fold paper, dot-matrix, bound in cable-wax — and Osy's hands go still on the wrapping, because the Continuum's last press was slagged with the mooring eight years ago; she stood witness; she signed the account whole.

Ledger grammar. Entries dated this spring. Purchases — small, kind, exact: a flooded lane in the tent rows drained a day before the fever season; a roof truss in the orphans' row renewed a week before the storm that would have tested it. Sequence numbers in a phase-offset scheme belonging to no reading of anyone. And the authorization, annotated — double-dash, carat, boxed question mark — in the hand of a man eight years dead, whose pen she watched burn, whose ledger she watched burn, whose ashes she scattered herself on the one true day's last morning, from the roof of the doorless barn, into a wind that was finally only wind.

She sits down at the Scale's table. The old dog leans his whole weight against her legs — the testament, executed daily for eight years — and looks up at her, and she looks down at him, the two remaining custodians of the completest file the Order ever kept, and she says, out loud, in the flat voice, to the dog, to the market, to the mild and unedited morning that has just stopped being either:

'Fetch my notebook, someone. The file is still open.'

epi_loop.text:
The city runs on four different days, and you have lived in the one where it is always almost autumn for — you check the tally marks, and the tally marks have stopped agreeing with your bones — some years now.

You never walked north. That is the one divergence the file cannot account for: item seven, uncrossed, has outlived its own schedule by years, and the reason lies across your doorway every morning at the point of maximum inconvenience, older now, grey to the eyebrows, thirty-four kilos of refusal. B has not crossed the threshold of your room in all these years. He guards it from outside. He eats, he patrols, he performs his offices — and he watches you, always, with his chin on his paws, the way a working dog watches a gate: as if something in you were a door that must not be allowed to open from the inside.

You have built things, because you are sad, and it is what you do. The receiver. The seam map. And, these last two years, the thing you cannot explain building: a listening rig pointed not at the horizon but down, at the transit mesh, sweeping, endlessly, for a voice you have no memory of ever hearing — sixty-one seconds, something in you insists, with the fluency of a man reciting stairs in the dark; listen for the cycle of sixty-one seconds — and finding, in all these years, nothing. The tunnels are quiet. The tunnels have always been quiet, the neighbors say. There was never any woman broadcasting east of here, the traders say. You have a folder of their sayings. On its cover, in your own impatient hand, you have written, without remembering choosing the words: THE RECORD IS CLEAN. THE RECORD IS NOT THE TRUTH.

And then, in an autumn that is almost, the old dog opens the file. You wake to find him standing — planted, geological — over a strip of paper on the workshop floor that was not there at midnight, in a room whose door he has spent years refusing: fan-fold, dot-matrix, weighted under cable-wax. Your hands know the paper before your eyes do. Ledger grammar: entries, current, dated this month — purchases, small and cold and exact, numbered in a phase-offset scheme, authorized in your own handwriting — and beneath them, not printed but written, pressed hard, in a hand that is yours down to the impatient crossbar, three lines no reading of you remembers writing:

'The mesh is jammed at the source. She broadcasts every day. You are 49, and the letter they will tell you doesn't exist is in a room your palm can open, and the dog knows the way, and has always known, and has been waiting for you to be worth it.

'— The file is still open.'

B looks at the strip, and then at you, and then — for the first time in all the almost-autumn years — he picks up his lead from its hook by the door, and carries it across the workshop, and lays it at your feet. And sits. Tail moving. Slow, then faster, like a second hand starting up, like a clock that has finally, finally, been given the correct time.
